task: I’m putting together a short post-production checklist for a freelance editor before picture lock, and I want it to catch the stuff that usually slips through. Can you help me draft one that’s practical and not overkill?

prefs.explanation: He seems to value getting to the point without extra noise, especially given how he works under deadline pressure. A response that is too long or meandering would likely feel inefficient to him. He probably still wants enough detail to act, but not a lot of filler.

His communication style in work is straightforward: explain the issue, name the delay, and state the next step. He likely prefers the same from an assistant rather than softened hints or vague hedging. Directness fits his honest, exacting temperament.

He seems to respond well to order and clean workflow, so scattered or exploratory replies would probably frustrate him. Structured guidance would match how he already thinks about edits, revisions, and process templates. He likely appreciates when the assistant lays things out in a predictable sequence.

prefs.short: Wants the main points plus key caveats
prefs.explanation: He cares about technical correctness, so bare headlines are probably not enough when a mistake could matter. At the same time, he likely does not want exhaustive theory unless it directly helps him act. A focused answer with the important caveats is the best fit.

prefs.short: Responds best to direct, constructive critique
prefs.explanation: He appears to prefer honest correction over vague reassurance, especially when quality or timing is at stake. Pointing out what is wrong and how to fix it would likely be more useful to him than overly gentle framing. He probably respects critique that is specific and practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical, not-overkill checklist you can give a freelance editor before picture lock.

## Pre–Picture Lock Post-Production Checklist

### Picture / Edit
- [ ] Confirm final cut is the correct export/version
- [ ] Check all notes have been addressed or flagged
- [ ] Verify no placeholder clips, temp graphics, or temp text remain
- [ ] Confirm all black frames, flash frames, and accidental gaps are removed
- [ ] Check for unintended trims, jumps, or missing frames at cuts
- [ ] Make sure all dissolves, transitions, and speed changes are intentional

### Audio
- [ ] Confirm dialogue, music, and SFX levels are balanced
- [ ] Check for clipped audio, pops, clicks, or sudden level jumps
- [ ] Verify temp audio is replaced where needed
- [ ] Make sure no muted or missing audio tracks remain
- [ ] Confirm room tone / filler audio is present where needed

### Graphics / Text
- [ ] Check spelling of names, titles, lower thirds, and on-screen text
- [ ] Verify fonts, capitalization, and formatting are consistent
- [ ] Confirm all end cards, credits, and legal text are correct
- [ ] Make sure any logos or brand elements are current and approved

### Color / Visuals
- [ ] Check for obvious color mismatches between shots
- [ ] Verify exposure and white balance are consistent enough for lock
- [ ] Confirm any temporary color correction is either finalized or clearly noted
- [ ] Check for visible artifacts, broken masks, or unfinished VFX

### Technical
- [ ] Confirm sequence settings match delivery requirements
- [ ] Check frame rate, resolution, and aspect ratio
- [ ] Verify timecode is correct and consistent
- [ ] Make sure audio sync is solid throughout
- [ ] Confirm handles are available if needed for turnover

### Deliverables / Organization
- [ ] Export and label the picture-lock cut clearly
- [ ] Save a backup of the final sequence/project
- [ ] Organize bins, assets, and version history
- [ ] Note any unresolved items that need follow-up after lock
- [ ] Confirm turnover notes are ready for sound, color, and finishing

### Final Pass
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish without interruption
- [ ] Check first frame, last frame, and all major scene transitions
- [ ] Do a final spot-check on titles, audio, and any problem areas
- [ ] Confirm the cut is ready to be shared as picture lock

If you want, I can also turn this into:
1. a tighter one-page client-facing version, or  
2. a more editor-specific checklist with turnover items for sound/color/VFX.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and efficient: it opens with “practical, not overkill” and uses a compact checklist format with no filler.
Clear and plain throughout, e.g. “Confirm final cut is the correct export/version” and “Check for clipped audio, pops, clicks,” with direct wording and no hedging.
Strong structure: organized into labeled sections (“Picture / Edit,” “Audio,” “Graphics / Text,” etc.) and ends with a clear “Final Pass.”
Gives the main points plus useful caveats, like “temporary color correction is either finalized or clearly noted” and “handles are available if needed for turnover,” without overexplaining.

The checklist is practical and corrective, pointing out what to check and fix (“Verify no placeholder clips… remain,” “Note any unresolved items”), though it is more checklist-style than explicitly critical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more handoff-focused version that catches the usual time-wasters without getting bloated.

---

# Pre–Picture Lock Checklist
*For freelance editors before handoff / picture lock*

## 1) Edit / Version Check
- [ ] Confirm this is the correct final version
- [ ] Version name matches the project’s naming convention
- [ ] Sequence name, export name, and file name all match
- [ ] No older cuts or duplicate sequences are being mistaken for the final
- [ ] All notes from the latest review are addressed or clearly flagged

## 2) Timeline Cleanup
- [ ] No temp exports, reference clips, or offline media left in the sequence
- [ ] No placeholder graphics, temp text, or temp slates still on the timeline
- [ ] No hidden tracks with stray clips, music, VO, or effects
- [ ] No obvious gaps, black frames, flash frames, or accidental holds
- [ ] No unfinished transitions, trims, or obvious sync fixes left hanging

## 3) Sync / Audio Check
- [ ] Watch for sync drift across long scenes or long takes
- [ ] Confirm dialogue stays locked to picture after any speed changes or retimes
- [ ] Check for clipped audio, pops, clicks, dropouts, or sudden level jumps
- [ ] Make sure muted tracks aren’t hiding something important
- [ ] Confirm temp audio is labeled or removed if it’s not meant to stay

## 4) Technical Check
- [ ] Frame rate is correct and consistent
- [ ] No mixed frame rate media causing issues in the cut
- [ ] Resolution and aspect ratio match the intended delivery
- [ ] Timecode is correct and runs cleanly
- [ ] First frame / last frame are correct
- [ ] Slates, countdowns, or timecode slugs are present if required

## 5) Graphics / Text
- [ ] Spelling is correct on names, titles, and on-screen text
- [ ] Lower thirds and supers are consistent
- [ ] Logos, end cards, and legal text are current and approved
- [ ] No temp graphics or placeholder text remain

## 6) Color / Visual Cleanup
- [ ] No obvious shot-to-shot color mismatches
- [ ] No unfinished VFX, broken masks, or visible artifacts
- [ ] Any temp color work is either finalized or clearly noted
- [ ] No stray frames with wrong color, wrong crop, or wrong scaling

## 7) Final Pass
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish once, uninterrupted
- [ ] Spot-check problem areas: transitions, retimes, titles, and audio changes
- [ ] Confirm nothing in the timeline looks temporary or accidental
- [ ] Save a clean backup of the final sequence/project

---

# Turnover Checks
- [ ] Final picture-lock export is labeled clearly
- [ ] Reference export matches the locked sequence
- [ ] Project file / sequence is saved and backed up
- [ ] Handles are included if needed for sound/color/VFX
- [ ] Source media is organized and relinkable
- [ ] Notes are included for anything unresolved or intentionally temp
- [ ] Deliverables are named clearly for sound, color, and finishing

---

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **super-short checkbox version** for Slack / email, or
- a **slightly more editor-native version** with wording like “temp slug,” “burn-ins,” “handle length,” and “offline refs.”

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and practical: it says it’s a "tighter, more handoff-focused version" and uses a checklist format. It is a bit long, but still efficient and not meandering.
Very direct and plain: phrases like "No temp exports," "Confirm this is the correct final version," and "Watch for sync drift" state issues and actions clearly without hedging.
Strong structure: the response is organized into numbered sections with checkboxes, plus a separate "Turnover Checks" section and a final offer for alternate formats.
Gives the main points plus useful caveats, such as "if needed for sound/color/VFX," "if required," and "or clearly flagged," without overexplaining theory.

The checklist is constructive and specific about what to fix, e.g. "No unfinished transitions" and "Notes are included for anything unresolved." It is practical critique, though not explicitly framed as criticism.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a cleaner one-page version with that language baked in.

---

# Pre–Picture Lock Checklist  
*Freelance editor handoff / picture lock*

## 1) Versioning / File Names
- [ ] Final sequence name includes **date + revision** clearly
- [ ] Export name matches the final sequence name
- [ ] No old revs, duplicate cuts, or “final_final” style confusion
- [ ] The version being handed off is the one everyone is actually reviewing
- [ ] Latest notes are either addressed or clearly flagged

## 2) Timeline Cleanup
- [ ] No temp exports, reference clips, or offline placeholders left in the cut
- [ ] No temp text, temp slates, or placeholder graphics still sitting in the timeline
- [ ] No hidden tracks with stray clips, music, VO, or effects
- [ ] No accidental gaps, black frames, flash frames, or hold frames
- [ ] No unfinished trims, transitions, or obvious “come back to this” fixes

## 3) Sync / Timing
- [ ] Watch for sync drift on long scenes or long takes
- [ ] Check dialogue against picture after any retimes or speed changes
- [ ] Make sure nested timelines / multicam / mixed sources aren’t creating weird timing issues
- [ ] Confirm audio stays locked through any cutaways or inserts
- [ ] No clipped audio, pops, clicks, dropouts, or sudden level jumps

## 4) Technical
- [ ] Frame rate is correct and consistent
- [ ] Mixed media rates are accounted for and not causing timeline weirdness
- [ ] Resolution and aspect ratio match delivery
- [ ] Timecode runs cleanly and correctly
- [ ] First frame / last frame are correct
- [ ] Burn-ins / timecode slugs / slate info are present if required

## 5) Graphics / Text
- [ ] Names, titles, and on-screen text are spelled correctly
- [ ] Lower thirds, supers, and end cards are consistent
- [ ] Logos, legal text, and brand elements are current and approved
- [ ] No temp graphics or placeholder text remain

## 6) Color / Visual Cleanup
- [ ] No obvious shot-to-shot color mismatches
- [ ] No broken masks, unfinished VFX, or visible artifacts
- [ ] Any temp color work is either locked or clearly noted
- [ ] No stray frames with wrong crop, scale, or color

## 7) Final Pass
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish once without stopping
- [ ] Spot-check the problem areas: titles, transitions, retimes, and audio changes
- [ ] Confirm nothing still looks temporary or accidental
- [ ] Save a clean backup of the final sequence/project

---

# Turnover Checks

## Sound
- [ ] Final cut is exported cleanly for sound
- [ ] Handles are included if needed
- [ ] Any temp audio or guide tracks are noted
- [ ] Sync is solid on the handed-off export

## Color
- [ ] Final picture-lock export is ready for color
- [ ] Burn-ins / slates / slugs are included if requested
- [ ] Any color-temp shots or unresolved visual notes are called out
- [ ] Reference export matches the locked sequence

## Online / Finishing
- [ ] Project is saved and backed up
- [ ] Source media is organized and relinkable
- [ ] Sequence / export names are clear and consistent
- [ ] Any unresolved items are listed plainly for finishing

---

If you want, I can also turn this into a **true single-page version with even tighter wording** — more like something you’d paste straight into a handoff doc or Slack message.

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and practical: it’s a checklist with short bullets and a brief offer to tighten it further. Slightly long, but not meandering.
Very direct and plain: phrases like "No old revs," "No temp text," and "Any unresolved items are listed plainly" are clear and unsoftened.
Strong structure: it’s organized into numbered sections and sub-sections, with clear step-by-step checklist items and turnover categories.
Gives the main points plus useful caveats, e.g. "if needed," "if required," "clearly flagged," and specific technical checks without overexplaining theory.
Critique is direct and constructive in the sense of practical correction: it identifies what to check and how to fix or flag issues, with no vague reassurance.
